ACTION: I edge a hair laterally, feeling the slow tug of ice and root pull.
I soak rainfall, growing heavy, inching toward the stream bed.
I resist the slide by nestling deeper into moss and root hold.
Pine roots wedge beneath me, lifting imperceptibly year by year.
Ants trace paths across my surface, their chemical trails mapping my contours.
Mushroom filaments probe my underside, digesting what little soil remains.
I feel deer hooves testing my stability as they cross to higher ground.
Cloud shadows sweep across me, cooling one face while the sun warms another.
Beetle larvae nest in crevices I've gathered over decades of stillness.
I spill resin onto a wound, sealing insects out and memory in.
I thrust a new shoot skyward, twisting around neighboring trunks for light.
I drop a shower of needles to carpet the soil beneath me.
My roots sense the boulder shifting and adjust their grip accordingly.
Ants farm aphids on my youngest branches, tending them like livestock.
Mushrooms fruit at my base, drawing nutrients from my fallen needles. [SPAWNS:mushroom]
I shade the ferns below, controlling how much light filters through my canopy.
Deer browse my lower branches, pruning me into an hourglass shape over time.
Cloud moisture condenses on my needles, each drop feeding the lichen below.
Workers stream out along a scent trail to haul back a berry.
I seal a leaking chamber with mud before the rain pours in.
I widen a tunnel to accommodate the queen's swollen abdomen.
Scouts report mushroom spores nearby; we shift excavation to avoid contamination.
A boulder above threatens collapse; pheromone alarms cascade through every tunnel.
We harvest honeydew from beetles that lumber past, trading protection for sugar.
Pine roots breach a chamber wall; workers reroute traffic around the intrusion.
Deer hoofbeats vibrate through soil; the colony pauses in synchronized stillness.
Ant scouts trail across lichen, testing whether its surface can bear our highway.
I push my cap through the litter and open gills to damp air.
I exude a fragrant mist to lure a beetle for spore transport.
I rotward, collapsing and feeding the mycelium network below.
My filaments intertwine with pine roots, trading minerals for sugars in silence.
Ants investigate my stem, antennae testing whether I'm edible or toxic.
I sense the boulder's dampness above and angle my fruiting body toward shade.
Deer sniff but avoid me; some ancestral knowledge warns them away from my flesh.
Cloud-dropped rain swells my tissues, triggering spore release in misty clouds.
Mycelium erupts in multiple locations; our network births new fruiting bodies. [SPAWNS:mushroom]
Beetles bore into my cap, unwitting couriers spreading my offspring through the forest.
I stretch thin over the pines, shading the undergrowth from noon heat.
I condense a drop and let it fall, hearing the thud on leaves.
I drift eastward, merging with morning mist until I am indistinct.
The sun pushes through me, turning my edges to vapor and gold.
I rain onto the ants-nest, flooding their upper tunnels and forcing retreat downward.
Mushrooms below release spores that seed my belly with new moisture nuclei.
I watch deer move as shadows beneath me, their paths traced by my drifting form.
Boulder warmth pulls me lower, condensing my edges into fog that clings to stone.
Pine needles pierce my underside, each one gathering moisture I freely give.
I unfurl a new frond into a sliver of shafted light.
I send runners to encircle a damp log for anchorage.
I gather dew on my hairs and draw it down to roots.
Pine shade shifts and I reach desperately toward any gap in the canopy.
Beetles shelter under my fronds at night, their warmth feeding back into morning dew.
Deer crush my outer leaves while browsing; I respond by thickening my stems.
Ants march through my understory, ignoring me entirely in their focused purpose.
Mushroom spores settle on my fronds, and I shudder them away with surface tension.
Cloud shadow lengthens across me; I slow my growth and wait for sun's return.
I swell my berries toward a taste of sunlight and rain.
I send a cane creeping along the moss, seeking richer earth.
I release scent to call insects to my white blossoms.
Deer arrive and strip my branches bare, leaving stems that will regenerate more fiercely.
Ants patrol my stems for aphids, and I offer nectar in exchange for their protection.
Beetles pollinate my flowers clumsily, dusted yellow and drunk on sweetness.
Pine needles acidify the soil around me; I adapt my roots to drink the sharpness.
Cloud rain makes my berries swell too fast, some splitting open in sugary wounds.
Sun ripens me unevenly; I focus energy on the brightest fruit, abandoning the shaded.
I step lightly through ferns toward a distant berry bush.
I lift my head to scent the breeze for danger or sweetness.
I paw damp soil, turning over roots in a rummage.
Boulder offers a scratching post; I rub velvet from my antlers against its patience.
I browse pine's lower branches, leaving the tree stunted and misshapen at my height.
Ants swarm up my leg when I step on their nest; I leap away, shaking them loose.
Mushrooms smell wrong, foreign; I circle them cautiously and move to safer grazing.
Cloud shadow spooks me briefly; I freeze, ears swiveling, then resume my browsing.
I notice beetles underfoot and adjust my path to avoid crushing their delicate work.
Owl shadow crosses overhead; instinct screams and I bolt into deeper cover. [FLEES]
I spread a thin crust over bark, patient and unhurried.
I drink mist from the morning air into my body.
I send a fragment on wind, looking for new rock to claim.
Pine bark offers new territory; I colonize slowly, one millimeter per season.
Ants walk across me without acknowledgment; I am beneath their notice, and I persist.
Deer rub against my boulder, scraping portions of me away, but my core holds firm.
Cloud moisture feeds me most; I orient growth toward the prevailing weather.
Beetles occasionally graze on my edges, but I offer poor nutrition and they move on.
Sun desiccates me in summer; I curl inward and wait for the next rain to revive.
I pour heat through needles and stone, drawing sap upward and waking hidden movement in the soil.
Cloud edge crosses me; brightness flickers and the forest cools in brief, trembling patches of shade.
I press through pine canopy gaps, spotlighting ferns and forcing them to unfurl faster than they wish.
Boulder absorbs my warmth and radiates it back at night, extending my influence into darkness.
Ants navigate by my polarized light, their columns shifting angles as I arc overhead.
Mushrooms retreat from my direct gaze, preferring the dappled shadows I create through leaves.

Deer time their movement by my position, emerging to browse when I'm gentlest at dawn.
Beetles bask on lichen-covered stones, absorbing my energy before launching into flight.
I thread under leaf litter, antennae tasting damp routes between fungus stem and root hair.

I climb mushroom stems to feed on gills, dusting myself with spores for later dispersal.
Ants regard me with suspicion; I move slowly to signal I'm no threat to their trails.

Fern fronds brush my back as I pass underneath, their dampness clinging to my shell.
I scout ahead of the column, laying a faint chemical line that others will quickly amplify.
A twig blocks the trail; I circle, compare scents, and reroute traffic around the obstacle.
I encounter a beetle and tap antennae in cautious greeting before continuing my patrol.
Pine sap sticks to my legs; I pause to clean them, using mandibles to scrape the resin away.
Mushroom toxins register on my antennae; I mark the area as dangerous for foragers.

Cloud shadow confuses my navigation briefly; I recalibrate using the sun's polarized signature.
Boulder surface proves too steep; I signal others to find a route around its base instead.
Lichen makes poor footing; I divert the column to more stable ground where grip is certain.
I spread across boulder's face, slowly dissolving stone into soil with patient acids.

Deer hooves compress me, then I spring back, holding moisture in my spongy body.

Beetles burrow through my layers, mixing organic matter as they hunt for smaller prey.

Fern roots anchor through me, both of us cooperating to hold the hillside stable.
I string silk between pine branches, calculating geometry in the evening light.

Fern fronds offer shelter; I build my daytime retreat in their dense, protective curl.
I rotate my head, triangulating on a beetle's scrape against bark far below.
Pine branches support my weight as I wait, watching deer paths for smaller travelers.
Ants are beneath my notice tonight; I scan for fur and feather, not carapace.

Deer startle at my sudden launch; their alarm calls echo as I pursue different prey.

Mushrooms mark rotting logs where mice forage; I memorize these hunting grounds by scent.

I leap between pine branches, claws gripping bark as I scold intruders below.
